TEXT.English: The Baha'i Faith is a monotheistic religion founded by Baha'u'llah in nineteenth-century Persia, emphasizing the spiritual unity of all humankind. There are an estimated five to six million Baha'i's around the world in more than 200 countries and territories. Baha'i teachings emphasize the underlying unity of the major world religions. Religious history is seen to have unfolded through a series of divine messengers, each of whom established a religion that was suited to the needs of the time and the capacity of the people. These messengers have included Abraham, Krishna, Buddha, Jesus, Muhammad, and others, including most recently Baha'u'lah. In Baha'i belief, each messenger taught of the next, and Baha'u'lah's life and teachings fulfill the end-time promises of previous scriptures. Humanity is understood to be involved in a process of collective evolution, and the need of the present time is for the gradual establishment of peace, justice, and unity on a global scale.
TEXT.French: Le Bahaรฏsme i la foi est une religion monotheistic fondรฉe par Baha' u' llah en dix-neuviรจme-siรจcle Perse, soulignant l'unitรฉ spirituelle de toute l'humanitรฉ. Il y a cinq ร six millions de Bahaรฏsme environ i' s autour du monde dans plus de 200 pays et territoires. Bahaรฏsme les enseignements i soulignent l'unitรฉ fondamentale des religions principales du monde. L'histoire religieuse est vue pour avoir dรฉvoilรฉ par une sรฉrie de messagers divins, chacune de qui a รฉtabli une religion qui a รฉtรฉ adaptรฉe aux besoins du temps et ร la capacitรฉ des personnes. Ces messagers ont inclus Abraham, Krishna, Bouddha, Jรฉsus, Muhammad et d'autres, incluant rรฉcemment Bahaรฏsme u' lah. Dans Bahaรฏsme croyance i, chaque messager enseignรฉ du prochain, et Bahaรฏsme u' lah' la vie et les enseignements de s accomplissent les promesses d'extrรฉmitรฉ-temps des รฉcritures saintes prรฉcรฉdentes. On comprend que l'humanitรฉ est impliquรฉe dans un processus d'รฉvolution collective, et le besoin de l'รฉpoque actuelle est pour l'รฉtablissement progressif de la paix, de la justice et de l'unitรฉ sur une รฉchelle globale.
TEXT.German: Das Bahai I ist Glaube eine monotheistic Religion, die durch Baha' gegrรผndet wird u' llah im 19.-Jahrhundert Persien, die geistige Einheit aller Menschheit hervorhebend. Es gibt geschรคtzten fรผnf bis sechs Million Bahai i' s um die Welt in mehr als 200 Lรคndern und in Gegenden. Bahai Unterricht I hebt die zugrunde liegende Einheit der Hauptweltreligionen hervor. Fromme Geschichte wird gesehen, durch eine Reihe Boten Gottes, jede von ausgebreitet zu haben, stellte wem eine Religion her, die zu den Notwendigkeiten der Zeit und zur Kapazitรคt der Leute entsprochen wurde. Diese Kuriere haben Abraham, Krishna, Buddha, Jesus, Muhammad und andere miteingeschlossen und Bahai kรผrzlich mit eingeschlossen u' lah. In Bahai Glaube I, jeder Kurier unterrichtet vom folgenden und Bahai u' lah' das s-Leben und der Unterricht erfรผllt die Endezeit Versprechungen der vorhergehenden Schriften. Menschlichkeit wird verstanden, in einen Prozess der Kollektiventwicklung mit einbezogen zu werden, und die Notwendigkeit der Gegenwart ist fรผr die stufenweise Einrichtung des Friedens, der Gerechtigkeit und der Einheit auf einer globalen Skala.
TEXT.Italian: La Fede Bahรก'รญ รจ una religione monoteista fondata da Bahรก'u'llรกh in Persia del XIX secolo, sottolineando l'unitร spirituale di tutta l'umanitร . Ci sono circa 7.000.000 di seguaci Baha'i di tutto il mondo in piรน di 200 paesi e territori. Gli insegnamenti bahรก'รญ sottolineano l'unitร di fondo delle principali religioni del mondo. Secondo questa religione la storia viene spiegata da una serie di Messaggeri divini, ognuno dei quali ha stabilito una religione che รจ stata adeguata alle esigenze del tempo e la capacitร delle persone. Questi messaggeri comprendevano Abramo, Krishna, Buddha, Gesรน, Maometto e altri, tra cui il piรน recente Baha'u'lah. In fede Baha'i, ogni messaggero parlava del proprio successore, e la vita e gli insegnamenti di Baha'u'lah mantengono le promesse del tempo della fine di scritture precedenti. L'umanitร รจ coinvolta in un processo di evoluzione collettiva, e la necessitร del tempo presente รจ per l'istituzione progressiva di pace, di giustizia e di unitร su scala globale.
TEXT.Spanish: El Bahaรญsmo i la fe es una religiรณn monoteรญsta fundada por Bahaรญsmo u' llah en el diecinueveavo-siglo Persia, acentuando la unidad espiritual de toda la humanidad. Hay cinco a seis millones de Bahaรญsmo estimados i' s en todo el mundo en mรกs de 200 paรญses y territorios. Bahaรญsmo las enseรฑanzas i acentรบan la unidad subyacente de las religiones principales del mundo. La historia religiosa se considera para haber revelado con una serie de mensajeros divinos, cada uno de quiรฉn estableciรณ una religiรณn que fue adaptada a las necesidades del tiempo y a la capacidad de la gente. Estos mensajeros han incluido Abraham, Krishna, Buddha, Jesรบs, Mohamed y a otros, incluyendo recientemente Bahaรญsmo u' lah. En Bahaรญsmo creencia i, cada mensajero enseรฑado del siguiente, y Bahaรญsmo u' lah' la vida y las enseรฑanzas de s satisfacen las promesas del fin-tiempo de escrituras anteriores. La humanidad se entiende para ser implicada en un proceso de la evoluciรณn colectiva, y la necesidad del actual tiempo estรก para el establecimiento gradual de paz, de justicia y de unidad en una escala global.
TEXT.Polish: Bahaizm - religia monoteistyczna, za³o¿ona w Persji w XIX wieku przez Bahรก'u'llรกha, podkreœlaj¹ca duchow¹ jednoœæ ca³ej ludzkoœci. Szacuje siê, ¿e obecnie na œwiecie jest oko³o piêciu do szeœciu milionów bahaitów w ponad 200 krajach i terytoriach. Jako ruch religijny, bahaizm wy³oni³ siê z babizmu. W trakcie rozwoju z bahaizmu wyodrêbni³o siê kilka wyznañ. Obecnie kilkanaœcie wyznañ uznaje siê za wywodz¹ce siê z pierwotnej nauki Bahรก'u'llรกha. Znaczna czêœæ poni¿szego has³a dotyczy zdecydowanie najwiêkszego, pod wzglêdem liczebnoœci wyznawców, wyznania bahaickiego z centrum administracyjnym i duchowym w Hajfie.
TEXT.Russian: รร รตร รจ (รกร รตร รจรงรฌ, รกรฅรตร รจรงรฌ) (ร รฐร รก. ''รร รตร '' - ''รฑรซร รขร '', ''รฑรขรฅรฒ'', ''รกรซรฅรฑรช'', ''รขรฅรซรจรชรฎรซรฅรฏรจรฅ'') - รฌรฎรญรฎรฒรฅรจรฑรฒรจรทรฅรฑรชร รฟ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฟ. ร รฅ รฎรฑรญรฎรขร รฒรฅรซรผ รร รตร รณรซรซร (1817-1892) รฏรฎรทรจรฒร รฅรฒรฑรฟ รชร รช รฏรฎรฑรซรฅรครญรจรฉ รข รฐรฟรครณ ''รฟรขรซรฅรญรจรฉ รรฎรฃร '', รชรฎรฒรฎรฐรปรฉ, รฏรฎรฌรจรฌรฎ รร รตร รณรซรซรป, รขรชรซรพรทร รฅรฒ รรขรฐร ร รฌร , รรฎรจรฑรฅรฟ, รรณรครครณ, รร รฐร รฒรณรฑรฒรฐรณ, รรฐรจรธรญรณ, รรจรฑรณรฑร รรฐรจรฑรฒร , รรณรตร รฌรฌรฅรคร , รร รกร . รรฑรฅรฌรจรฐรญรปรฉ รถรฅรญรฒรฐ รญร รตรฎรครจรฒรฑรฟ รข รร รฉรดรฅ. รรทรฅรญรจรฅ รกร รตร รจ รฎรฑรญรฎรขร รญรฎ รญร รณรทรฅรญรจรฟรต รร รกร รจ รรจรฐรงรป รรณรฑรฅรฉรญ รรซรจ (รญร รงรขร รขรธรฅรฃรฎรฑรฟ รร รตร รณรซรซรฎรฉ). รรซร รขรญรปรฅ รฒรฅรฌรป: รฅรครจรญรฑรฒรขรฎ รรฎรฃร , รฅรครจรญรฑรฒรขรฎ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฉ รจ รฅรครจรญรฑรฒรขรฎ รทรฅรซรฎรขรฅรทรฅรฑรฒรขร . รร รฏรฎรฌรจรญร รฟ รฎรก ร รญร รซรฎรฃรจรทรญรปรต รณรทรฅรญรจรฟรต รฏรฐรฅรฆรญรจรต รฟรขรซรฅรญรจรฉ รรฎรฃร , รร รตร รณรซรซร รงร รฟรขรซรฟรฅรฒ, รทรฒรฎ รฑรณรนรฅรฑรฒรขรณรฅรฒ รฒรฎรซรผรชรฎ รฎรครญร รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฟ - ''รญรฅรจรงรฌรฅรญรญร รฟ รขรฅรฐร รรฎรฆรจรฟ, รขรฅรทรญร รฟ รข รฏรฐรฎรธรซรฎรฌ, รขรฅรทรญร รฟ รข รฃรฐรฟรครณรนรฅรฌ'', ร รญร รซรฎรฃรจรทรญรฎ รฒรฎรฌรณ, รชร รช รข รรฎรฐร รญรฅ ''รจรฑรซร รฌรฎรฌ'' รญร รงรปรขร รฅรฒรฑรฟ รซรพรกร รฟ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฟ รฎรฒรชรฐรฎรขรฅรญรจรฟ. รร รตร รจ รฑรทรจรฒร รพรฒ รฑรขรฎรพ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรพ รฏรฎรฑรซรฅรครญรฅรฉ รฌรจรฐรฎรขรฎรฉ รฌรฎรญรฎรฒรฅรจรฑรฒรจรทรฅรฑรชรฎรฉ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฅรฉ รฎรฒรชรฐรฎรขรฅรญรจรฟ, รข รฒรฎ รขรฐรฅรฌรฟ รชร รช รฑรฐรฅรครจ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฎรขรฅรครฎรข รฅรฅ รชรซร รฑรฑรจรดรจรถรจรฐรณรพรฒ รข รครจร รฏร รงรฎรญรฅ รฎรฒ รจรฑรซร รฌรฑรชรฎ-รฑรจรญรชรฐรฅรฒรจรทรฅรฑรชรฎรฉ รฑรฅรชรฒรป รครฎ รญรฎรขรฎรฉ รฌรจรฐรฎรขรฎรฉ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรจ.[PARAGRAPH:1]รรทรฅรญรจรฅ รกร รตร รจ รจรญรฎรฃรคร รฐร รฑรฑรฌร รฒรฐรจรขร รพรฒ รข รชร รทรฅรฑรฒรขรฅ รฑรจรญรชรฐรฅรฒรจรทรฅรฑรชรฎรฉ รชรฎรฌรกรจรญร รถรจรจ รฏรฐรฅรครธรฅรฑรฒรขรณรพรนรจรต รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฎรงรญรปรต รขรฅรฐรฎรขร รญรจรฉ. รรฎรฒรฟ รจรงรญร รทร รซรผรญรฎ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฎรงรญรฎรฅ รฒรฅรทรฅรญรจรฅ รขรฎรงรญรจรชรซรฎ รข รฐรณรฑรซรฅ รธรจรจรฒรฑรชรฎรฃรฎ รจรฑรซร รฌร , รฑรฅรฃรฎรครญรฟ รกรฎรซรผรธรจรญรฑรฒรขรฎ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฎรขรฅรครฎรข รฑรทรจรฒร รพรฒ, รทรฒรฎ รขรฅรฐร รกร รตร รจ รฑรฒร รซร รฑร รฌรฎรฑรฒรฎรฟรฒรฅรซรผรญรฎรฉ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฅรฉ. รรฎ รฌรญรฅรญรจรพ รกร รตร รจ, รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฟ รขรฑรฅรถรฅรซรฎ รฎรฑรญรฎรขร รญร รญร รณรทรฅรญรจรจ รฅรฅ รฎรฑรญรฎรขรฎรฏรฎรซรฎรฆรญรจรชร รร รตร รณรซรซรป. รรณรนรฅรฑรฒรขรณรฅรฒ รฒรฎรทรชร รงรฐรฅรญรจรฟ, รทรฒรฎ รกร รตร รจ รญรฅ รฟรขรซรฟรฅรฒรฑรฟ รชรณรซรผรฒรฎรขรปรฌ รฎรฒรขรฅรฒรขรซรฅรญรจรฅรฌ, รฐรฅรดรฎรฐรฌรจรฑรฒรฑรชรจรฌ รฒรฅรทรฅรญรจรฅรฌ รจรซรจ รฑรฅรชรฒรฎรฉ รข รฐร รฌรชร รต รชร รชรฎรฉ-รซรจรกรฎ รครฐรณรฃรฎรฉ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรจ, รญรฅรซรผรงรฟ รฅรฅ รญร รงรขร รฒรผ รจ รฏรฐรฎรฑรฒรฎ รดรจรซรฎรฑรฎรดรฑรชรฎรฉ รฑรจรฑรฒรฅรฌรฎรฉ. ร รจรฑรซร รฌรฅ รณรทรฅรญรจรฅ รฐร รฑรถรฅรญรจรขร รฅรฒรฑรฟ รชร รช รขรฅรฐรฎรฎรฒรฑรฒรณรฏรญรจรทรฅรฑรฒรขรฎ, รข รฑรขรฟรงรจ รฑ รทรฅรฌ รข รรฐร รญรฅ รจ รญรฅรชรฎรฒรฎรฐรปรต รครฐรณรฃรจรต รฌรณรฑรณรซรผรฌร รญรฑรชรจรต รฑรฒรฐร รญร รต รกร รตร รจ รฏรฎรครขรฅรฐรฃร รซรจรฑรผ รฏรฐรฅรฑรซรฅรครฎรขร รญรจรฟรฌ. รรฎรญรถรฅรฏรถรจรฟ รรฎรฃร รข รขรฅรฐรฅ รกร รตร รจ รฑรฒรฐรฎรฃรฎ รฌรฎรญรฎรฒรฅรจรฑรฒรจรทรญร รจ รฒรฐร รญรฑรถรฅรญรครฅรญรฒรญร . รรทรฅรญรจรฅ รกร รตร รจ รฑรทรจรฒร รฅรฒ รขรฑรฟรชรจรฅ รฏรฎรฏรปรฒรชรจ รฏรฎรงรญร รฒรผ รฑรณรนรญรฎรฑรฒรผ รรฎรฃร รกรฅรฑรฏรฎรซรฅรงรญรปรฌรจ (รฝรชรฑรฒรฐรฅรฌร รซรผรญรปรฉ รจรฐรฐร รถรจรฎรญร รซรจรงรฌ). ร รครจรญรฑรฒรขรฅรญรญรฎรฉ รฑรขรฟรงรผรพ รฌรฅรฆรครณ รรฎรฃรฎรฌ รจ รซรพรครผรฌรจ รกร รตร รจ รฑรทรจรฒร รพรฒ รรฎรฑรซร รญรญรจรชรฎรข รรฎรฃร (รรฐรฎรฐรฎรชรฎรข, รรขรจรฒรฅรซรฅรฉ), รชรฎรฒรฎรฐรปรต รฎรญรจ รญร รงรปรขร รพรฒ ''รรฎรฃรฎรฟรขรซรฅรญรจรฟรฌรจ''. รรฎรฃรคร รกร รตร รจ รฃรฎรขรฎรฐรฟรฒ, รทรฒรฎ รขรฑรฅ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรจ รฑรฎรฑรฒร รขรซรฟรพรฒ รฎรครญรฎ รถรฅรซรฎรฅ, รฝรฒรฎ รญรฅ รฎรงรญร รทร รฅรฒ, รทรฒรฎ รฐร รงรซรจรทรญรปรฅ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฎรงรญรปรฅ รณรทรฅรญรจรฟ รฏรฎรตรฎรฆรจ รครฐรณรฃ รญร รครฐรณรฃร . รรญรจ รขรฅรฐรฟรฒ, รทรฒรฎ รฑรณรนรฅรฑรฒรขรณรฅรฒ รฎรครญร รขรฅรฐร รข รรฎรฃร , รจ รขรฑรฅ รรฎรฑรซร รญรญรจรชรจ รรฎรฃร รฐร รฑรชรฐรปรขร รซรจ รฅรฅ รฑรณรนรญรฎรฑรฒรผ. รรฎรฃรซร รฑรญรฎ รครฎรชรฒรฐรจรญรฅ รกร รตร รจ, ''รครซรฟ รกร รตร รจ รจรญรครณรจรฑรฒรฑรชรฎรฃรฎ รฏรฐรฎรจรฑรตรฎรฆรครฅรญรจรฟ รร รตร รณรซรซร รฟรขรซรฟรฅรฒรฑรฟ รญรฎรขรฎรฉ รจรญรชร รฐรญร รถรจรฅรฉ รรฐรจรธรญรป. รรซรฟ รกร รตร รจ, รฟรขรซรฟรพรนรจรตรฑรฟ รขรปรตรฎรครถร รฌรจ รจรง รฑรฐรฅรครป รตรฐรจรฑรฒรจร รญ, รร รตร รณรซรซร รขรฎรฏรซรฎรนร รฅรฒ รฎรกรฅรนร รญรจรฅ รรฐรจรฑรฒร รขรฎรงรขรฐร รฒรจรฒรผรฑรฟ ?รขรฎ รฑรซร รขรฅ รรฒรถร ?, รฑรฎรฅรครจรญรจรฒรผ รขรฑรฅรต รซรพรครฅรฉ, รทรฒรฎรกรป รกรปรซรจ ?รฎรครญร รฏร รฑรฒรขร รจ รฎรครจรญ รฏร รฑรฒรปรฐรผ?''.[PARAGRAPH:2]รรฑรญรฎรขรญรปรฅ รฏรฐรจรญรถรจรฏรป รขรฅรฐรฎรณรทรฅรญรจรฟ รร รตร รณรซรซรป:[PARAGRAPH:1]1) รรฐรจรงรญร รญรจรฅ รฎรกรนรฅรฃรฎ รจรฑรฒรฎรทรญรจรชร รจ รญรฅรฎรฏรฐรฎรขรฅรฐรฆรจรฌรฎรฃรฎ รฅรครจรญรฑรฒรขร รขรฑรฅรต รฎรฑรญรฎรขรญรปรต รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฉ รฌรจรฐร ;[PARAGRAPH:1]2) รร รฌรฎรฑรฒรฎรฟรฒรฅรซรผรญรปรฉ รฏรฎรจรฑรช รจรฑรฒรจรญรป รชร รฆรครปรฌ รขรฅรฐรณรพรนรจรฌ;[PARAGRAPH:1]3) รรฒรชร รง รฎรฒ รขรฑรฅรต รขรจรครฎรข รฏรฐรฅรครฐร รฑรฑรณรครชรฎรข (รฐร รฑรฎรขรปรต, รญร รถรจรฎรญร รซรผรญรปรต, รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฎรงรญรปรต, รชรซร รฑรฑรฎรขรปรต, รฏรฎรซรจรฒรจรทรฅรฑรชรจรต);[PARAGRAPH:1]4) รรฐรจรงรญร รญรจรฅ รฒรฎรฃรฎ, รทรฒรฎ รจรฑรฒรจรญรญร รฟ รฐรฅรซรจรฃรจรฟ รขรฑรฅรฃรคร รญร รตรฎรครจรฒรฑรฟ รข รฃร รฐรฌรฎรญรจรจ รฑ รฐร รงรณรฌรฎรฌ รจ รญร รณรทรญรปรฌ รงรญร รญรจรฅรฌ;[PARAGRAPH:1]5) รรญร รทรจรฌรฎรฑรฒรผ รจ รญรฅรฎรกรตรฎรครจรฌรฎรฑรฒรผ รขรฑรฅรฎรกรนรฅรฃรฎ รฎรกรฐร รงรฎรขร รญรจรฟ;[PARAGRAPH:1]6) รรฑรณรนรฅรฑรฒรขรซรฅรญรจรฅ รฐร รขรญรฎรฏรฐร รขรจรฟ รฌรณรฆรทรจรญ รจ รฆรฅรญรนรจรญ;[PARAGRAPH:1]7) รรฑรฒรฐร รญรฅรญรจรฅ รชรฐร รฉรญรจรต รดรฎรฐรฌ รกรฅรครญรฎรฑรฒรจ รจ รกรฎรฃร รฒรฑรฒรขร ;[PARAGRAPH:1]8) TEXT.English: "O God! Grant Thy favour, and bestow Thy blessing. Vouchsafe Thy grace, and give a portion of Thy bounty. Send down Thy heavenly rain, and provide Thy plenteousness and abundance." - Abdul-Bah?
TEXT.French: "Oh mon dieu! Accorde ta faveur, et donner ta bรฉnรฉdiction. Accorde ta grรขce, et de donner une partie de ta gรฉnรฉrositรฉ? Envoyer bas ta pluie cรฉleste, et de fournir la multitude et l'abondance." - Abdul-Bahรก
TEXT.Spanish: "ยกOh Dios! Dame tu favor, y otรณrgame tu bendiciรณn. Concรฉdeme tu gracia, y entrรฉgame una porciรณn de tu belleza. Envรญame hacia aquรญ tu lluvia celestial, y proporciรณname tu abundancia y plenitud." - Abdul-Bahรก

TEXT.English: Buddhism is one of the world's major religions, with more that 350 million followers. Buddhism originated in India and has since spread throughout the subcontinent and into central and southeast Asia.[PARAGRAPH:2]Buddhism follows the teachings of Siddhartha Gautama, the Buddha. Gautama lived from 620 to 540 BC. The Buddha ("enlightened one") sought to teach his followers to search for "Dharma," the truth. By doing so, they too would become enlightened and thus achieve "Nirvana," the perfect peace that would free one's soul from the unending cycle of death and rebirth that all men suffer.[PARAGRAPH:2]Three major schools of Buddhism survive today. Vajrayana believes that people can "speed up" enlightenment through "tantric" techniques such as yoga, mantras (chants), and various other rituals. Theravada and Mahayana are sutric schools that believe that one achieves enlightenment primarily through study of the Budda's teachings.
TEXT.French: Le bouddhisme, avec plus de 350 millions de fidรจles, est considรฉrรฉ comme l'une des principales religions du monde. Originaire d'Inde, il s'est rรฉpandu dans tout le sous-continent ainsi qu'en Asie centrale et en Asie du Sud-Est.[PARAGRAPH:2]Le bouddhisme suit les enseignements de Siddhartha Gautama, le Bouddha, qui a vรฉcu de 620 ร 540 av. JC Bouddha (l'"Eveillรฉ") s'efforรงait d'enseigner ร ses adeptes de rechercher le "Dharma", la vรฉritรฉ. De cette maniรจre, ils s'รฉveilleraient ร leur tour et entreraient ainsi dans le "Nirvana," l'รฉtat de paix parfaite qui dรฉlivrerait leur รขme du cycle incessant de la mort et de la renaissance que tous les hommes doivent endurer.[PARAGRAPH:2]Le bouddhisme d'aujourd'hui a vu survivre trois doctrines principales. Selon le bouddhisme vajrayana, les adeptes peuvent "accรฉlรฉrer" leur รฉveil en recourant ร des techniques "tantriques" telles que le yoga, les mantras (chants) et divers autres rituels. Les bouddhismes theravada et mahayana constituent des doctrines sutriques qui considรจrent que l'รฉveil n'est possible qu'aprรจs avoir appris les enseignements de Bouddha.
TEXT.German: Der Buddhismus ist mit รผber 350 Millionen Glรคubigen eine der Hauptreligionen der Welt. Ausgehend von Indien ist er inzwischen in weiten Teilen Zentral- und Sรผdostasiens verbreitet.[PARAGRAPH:2]Der Buddhismus folgt der Lehre Siddhartha Gautamas (um 560 bis 480 vor Christus). Als Buddha (wรถrtlich: Erwachter, Erleuchteter) lehrte Gautama seine Anhรคnger das Dharma (die Lehre) und wurde damit der Begrรผnder des Buddhismus. Ziel des Buddhismus ist das "Nirwana", also ein Zustand, in dem es kein Leid mehr gibt und in dem der Mensch den ewigen Kreislauf von Tod und Wiederverkรถrperung hinter sich gelassen hat.[PARAGRAPH:2]Heute gibt es drei Hauptrichtungen des Buddhismus. Die Vajrayana-Schule lehrt, dass der Mensch die Erleuchtung durch "tantrische" Techniken, wie Yoga, Mantras (Gesรคnge) und andere Rituale, "beschleunigen" kann. Theravada und Mahayana sind Sutra-Schulen, die lehren, dass die Erleuchtung allein durch das Studium der Lehren Buddhas erreicht werden kann.
TEXT.Italian: Il Buddismo รจ una delle religioni piรน importanti del mondo, con oltre 350 milioni di seguaci. Il Buddismo รจ originario dell'India e si รจ poi diffuso in tutto il subcontinente e nell'Asia centrale e sudorientale.[PARAGRAPH:2]Il Buddismo segue gli insegnamenti di Siddhartha Gautaman, il Budda, che visse dal 620 al 540 a. C. Il Budda ("l'illuminato") tentรฒ di insegnare ai suoi seguaci come raggiungere il "Dharma", la ricerca della veritร . Solo cosรฌ รจ possibile diventare illuminati e raggiungere il "Nirvana", uno stato di pace eterna che libera l'anima degli uomini dal ciclo infinito di morte e rinascita.[PARAGRAPH:2]Al giorno d'oggi, esistono tre grandi scuole di Buddismo: il Vjrayana crede che gli individui possano "velocizzare" l'illuminazione attraverso delle tecniche "tantriche", come lo yoga, i mantra (canti) e altri rituali. Theravada e Mahayana sono differenti scuole che credono nella raggiunta dell'illuminazione attraverso lo studio degli insegnamenti di Budda.
TEXT.Spanish: El budismo, con mรกs de trescientos cincuenta millones de seguidores, es una de las religiones mรกs importantes. Se originรณ en la India, y desde entonces se ha propagado por todo el subcontinente y las regiones centrales y meridionales de Asia.[PARAGRAPH:2]El budismo sigue las enseรฑanzas de Siddhartha Gautama, el Buda. Gautama viviรณ entre los aรฑos 620 y 540 a. C. El Buda ("iluminado") enseรฑรณ a sus seguidores que habรญan de buscar el "Dharma," la verdad. Al hacerlo alcanzarรญan la iluminaciรณn, y con ella, el "Nirvana", la paz perfecta que liberarรญa su alma del interminable ciclo de muerte y renacimiento que sufren todos los hombres.[PARAGRAPH:2]En la actualidad existen tres grandes escuelas dentro del budismo. El vajrayana cree que es posible acelerar la iluminaciรณn empleando tรฉcnicas "tรกntricas" como el yoga, los mantras (cรกnticos) y otros rituales. El theravada y el mahayana creen que la iluminaciรณn se alcanza fundamentalmente por medio del estudio de las enseรฑanzas del Buda.

TEXT.English: Canaanite religion is the name for the group of Ancient Semitic religions practiced by the Canaanites living in the ancient Levant from at least the early Bronze Age through the first centuries of the Common Era.[PARAGRAPH:2]Canaanite religion was polytheistic, and in some cases monolatristic.
TEXT.Italian: Religione cananea รจ il nome delle antiche religioni semitiche praticate dai Cananei che abitavano in Medioriente dall'inizio dell'etร del bronzo fino ai primi secoli dell'era comune.[PARAGRAPH:2]La religione cananea era politeista, e in qualche casi monoteista.
TEXT.Polish: Religia kananejska - system wierzeñ ludów zamieszka³ych w drugim i pierwszym tysi¹cleciu p.n.e. na terenie Kanaanu. Jej zniekszta³cony obraz z helleñskimi nalecia³oœciami przedstawi³ w II wieku p.n.e. Filon. Wed³ug mitów syro-palestyñskich wszechœwiat narodzi³ siê z wiatru, który wia³ nad wodami. Pierwszym bogiem by³ Mot. Gwiazdy, niebo i ziemia wyklu³y siê z kosmicznego jaja. Pierwsze pokolenie bóstw narodzi³o siê z bogini morza Aszratum i Nieba. W miarê, jak rozwija³a siê religia, Aszratum ³¹czy³a siê kolejno z Elem i Baalem, co objaœnia ich prawo do w³adzy. TEXT.Russian: ะฅะฐะฝะฐะฐะฝัะบะพะน ัะตะปะธะณะธะตะน ะฟัะธะฝััะพ ะฝะฐะทัะฒะฐัั ัะพะฒะพะบัะฟะฝะพััั ะทะฐะฟะฐะดะฝะพ-ัะตะผะธััะบะธั ัะทััะตัะบะธั ะบัะปััะพะฒ, ะธะผะตะฒัะธั ัะฐัะฟัะพัััะฐะฝะตะฝะธะต ะฝะฐ ัะตััะธัะพัะธะธ ะัะตะฒะฝะตะณะพ ะะตะฒะฐะฝัะฐ, ะบะฐะบ ะผะธะฝะธะผัะผ, ะพั ัะฟะพั ะธ ัะฐะฝะฝะตะน ะฑัะพะฝะทั ะดะพ ะฟะตัะฒัั ะฒะตะบะพะฒ ะฝะฐัะตะน ััั.[PARAGRAPH:2]ะะพะปััะธะฝััะฒะพ ั ะฐะฝะฐะฐะฝัะบะธั ะบัะปััะพะฒ ะฑัะปะธ ะฟะพะปะธัะตะธััะธัะตัะบะธะผะธ ะปะพะบะฐะปัะฝัะผะธ ัะตะปะธะณะธัะผะธ (ั.ะต. ะฟะพะบะปะพะฝัะปะธัั ะผะฝะพะถะตััะฒั ะฑะพะณะพะฒ - ยซัะปะพั ะธะผยป), ะพะดะฝะฐะบะพ ะฒ ะฝะตะบะพัะพััั ัะปััะฐัั ะฒัััะตัะฐะปะธัั ะธ ะผะพะฝะพัะตะธััะธัะตัะบะธะต ะบัะปััั, ะฒัะพะดะต ะะฐะฐะปะฐ ะธ ะฏั ะฒะต. ะะฐะบ ะฟัะฐะฒะธะปะพ, ะบัะปัั ั ั ะฐะฝะฐะฐะฝะตะตะฒ ะพัะฟัะฐะฒะปัะปัั ะฟัะพัะตััะธะพะฝะฐะปัะฝะพะน ะบะฐััะพะน ะถัะตัะพะฒ, ะบะพัะพััะต ะทะฐะฝะธะผะฐะปะธ ะฟัะธะฒะธะปะตะณะธัะพะฒะฐะฝะฝะพะต ะฟะพะปะพะถะตะฝะธะต ะฒ ะพะฑัะตััะฒะต. ะัั ะธัะตะบัััะฐ ะฅัะฐะผะพะฒ ะฑะพะปััะธะฝััะฒะฐ ะทะฐะฟะฐะดะฝะพัะตะผะธััะบะธั ะฑะพะณะพะฒ ัะฒะธะปะฐัั ะฟัะพะพะฑัะฐะทะพะผ ั ัะฐะผะฐ ะกะพะปะพะผะพะฝะฐ, ะบะพัะพััะน ะฑัะป ะฟะพัััะพะตะฝ ะฟัะธ ะฐะบัะธะฒะฝะพะน ะฟะพะผะพัะธ ัะธััะบะธั ะธะฝะถะตะฝะตัะพะฒ. ะะดะฝะฐะบะพ ะพัะปะธัะธั ัะตะปะธะณะธะพะทะฝัะต ััะตะดะธ ะฝะฐัะพะดะพะฒ ะฅะฐะฝะฐะฐะฝะฐ ะผะพะณะปะธ ะฑััั ะบะฐัะดะธะฝะฐะปัะฝัะผะธ: ะธัะดะตะธ ะฟะพะบะปะพะฝัะปะธัั ะะดะธะฝะพะผั ะะพะณั, ะฐ ัะธะฝะธะบะธะนัั - ะะฐะฝัะตะพะฝั ะฑะพะณะพะฒ. ะัะพะฑะพ ัะธะฝะธะบะธะนัั ะฟะพัะธัะฐะปะธ ะกะฒััะตะฝะฝัะต ะะพัั. ะะดะฝะธะผ ะธะท ะฟะพัะปะตะดะฝะธั ะฐะดะตะฟัะพะฒ ัะธะฝะธะบะธะนัะบะพะน ัะตะปะธะณะธะธ ะฑัะป ะะตะปะธะพะณะฐะฑะฐะป, ะบะพัะพััะน ะพัะพะฑะพะต ะฒะฝะธะผะฐะฝะธะต ัะดะตะปัะป ัะพะปััะฝะพะผั ะบัะปััั ะธ ัะบััะฐัะธัะตัะบะพะผั ะฟะพะบะปะพะฝะตะฝะธั. ะะตัั ะพะฒะฝัะน ะฑะพะณ ะธะผะตะฝะพะฒะฐะปัั ะะฐะฐะป. ะ ะณะพะดะธะฝั ะฝะตััะฐััะธะน ะณัะพะทะฝัะน ะฑะพะณ ััะตะฑะพะฒะฐะป ะถะตััะฒะพะฟัะธะฝะพัะตะฝะธะน ะผะปะฐะดะตะฝัะตะฒ (ัะพัะตั). ะญัะฐ ะธะฟะพััะฐัั ะะฐะฐะปะฐ ะฑัะปะฐ ะธะทะฒะตััะฝะฐ ะบะฐะบ ะะตะปัะบะฐัั ะธะปะธ ะะพะปะพั . ะงะฐััะพ ะะตัั ะพะฒะฝัะน ะฑะพะณ ะฟัะธะพะฑัะตัะฐะป ัะตััั ัะพะปััะฝะพะณะพ ะฑะพะถะตััะฒะฐ. ะะฐััะดั ั ะฝะตะฑะตัะฝัะผ ะผัะถัะบะธะผ ะฑะพะถะตััะฒะพะผ ััะตะดะธ ั ะฐะฝะฐะฐะฝะตะตะฒ ะฟะพัะธัะฐะปะฐัั ะธ ะฑะพะณะธะฝั-ะผะฐัั ะััะฐััะฐ (ะฑะพะถะตััะฒะพ ัะธะดะพะฝัะบะพะต - 3 ะฆะฐั. 11:5), ะพะดะฝะพะน ะธะท ัะพัะผ ะฟะพะบะปะพะฝะตะฝะธั ะบะพัะพัะพะน ะฑัะปะฐ ั ัะฐะผะพะฒะฐั ะฟัะพััะธัััะธั - ะพะดะฝะพะบัะฐัะฝะฐั ะฟัะพะดะฐะถะฐ ัะฒะพะตะณะพ ัะตะปะฐ ะทะฐ ะดะตะฝัะณะธ, ะพัะดะฐะฒะฐะตะผัะต ะฒ ั ัะฐะผ ะะพะณะธะฝะธ.[PARAGRAPH:2]ะฅะฐะฝะฐะฐะฝัะบะฐั ัะตะปะธะณะธั ะฒ ัะตะปะพะผ ะฟัะตะดััะฐะฒะปัะปะฐ ัะพะฑะพะน ัะธะฝัะตะท ัะฐะทะปะธัะฝัั ะทะฐะฟะฐะดะฝะพัะตะผะธััะบะธั ััะฐะดะธัะธะน. ะะพัะตะฒะพะน ัะตะผะธััะบะธะน ะบัะปัั ะฝะตะฑะตัะฝัั ัะฒะตัะธะป (ัะพะปััะฝะพะณะพ ะฑัะบะพะณะพะปะพะฒะพะณะพ ะกะพะปะฝัะฐ ะธ ะถะตะฝัะบะพะน ะฑะพะณะธะฝะธ ะัะฝั) ะฝะฐัะปะฐะธะฒะฐะปัั ะฝะฐ ะผะฐััะธะฐัั ะฐะปัะฝัะน ะฝะตะพะปะธัะธัะตัะบะธะน ะบัะปัั ะะตะปะธะบะพะน ะะฐัะตัะธ (ัะพะดััะฒะตะฝะฝะพะน ะะธะฑะตะปะต) ะธ ะตะณะธะฟะตััะบะพะน ะฟะฐัะต ะัะธะดั ะธ ะัะธัะธัะฐ (ะะดะพะฝะธั).

TEXT.English: "Yet the children of Manasseh could not drive out the inhabitants of those cities; but the Canaanites would dwell in that land." - Bible
TEXT.French: "Pourtant, les enfants de Manassรฉ ne purent pas chasser les habitants de ces villes, et les Cananรฉens voulurent rester dans ce pays." - Bible
TEXT.Polish: "I dzieci Manaseha nie mog³y przegoniæ mieszkañców tych miast, ale Kaananejczycy i tak zamieszkali na tym terenie." - Bible
TEXT.Russian: ยซะะพ ััะฝั ะะฐะฝะฐััะธะธะฝั ะฝะต ะผะพะณะปะธ ะฒัะณะฝะฐัั ะถะธัะตะปะตะน ะณะพัะพะดะพะฒ ัะธั , ะธ ะฅะฐะฝะฐะฝะตะธ ะพััะฐะปะธัั ะถะธัั ะฒ ะทะตะผะปะต ัะตะนยป. - ะะธะฑะปะธั

TEXT.English: [PARAGRAPH:0]Cao Dai (or "High Tower", a Taoist epithet for the supreme god) is a syncretist Vietnamese religious movement with a strongly nationalist political character. Cao Dai draws upon ethical precepts from Confucianism, occult practices from Taoism, theories of karma and rebirth from Buddhism, and a hierarchical organization (including a pope) from Roman Catholicism. Its pantheon of saints includes such diverse figures as the Buddha, Confucius, Jesus Christ, Muhammad, Pericles, Julius Caesar, Joan of Arc, Victor Hugo, and Sun Yat-sen. In Cao Dai, God is represented as an eye in a triangle, a symbol that appears on the facades of the sect's temples. The religion's organization is patterned after that of Roman Catholicism, with a pope, cardinals, and archbishops. Worship involves elaborate rituals and festivals.[PARAGRAPH:2]In 1919 Ngo Van Chieu (q.v.), an administrator for the French in Indochina, received a communication from the supreme deity during a table-moving sรฉance. Chieu became the prophet of the new religion, which was formally established in 1926. A Cao Dai army was established in 1943 during the Japanese occupation of Indochina. After the war the Cao Dai was an effective force in national politics; it first supported, then opposed, Premier Ngo Dinh Diem. In 1955โ56 Diem disbanded the Cao Dai army and forced the sect's pope, Pham Cong Tac, into exile. After the communist takeover in 1975, Cao Dai was reportedly repressed by the government. Centers of worship were established in Vietnamese refugee communities abroad, however, and by the early 1990s Cao Dai was reported to have some two million adherents in Vietnam, Cambodia, France, and the United States. Headquarters of the religion are at Tay Ninh (q.v.), near Ho Chi Minh City (formerly Saigon).[PARAGRAPH:2]- Encyclopaedia Britannica
TEXT.Russian: [PARAGRAPH:0]ะะฐะพะดะฐะน (ะฒัะตัะฝ. ยซะะพะปััะพะน ะณะปะฐะทยป) - ัะธะฝะบัะตัะธัะตัะบะฐั ะผะพะฝะพัะตะธััะธัะตัะบะฐั ัะตะปะธะณะธั, ะพัะฝะพะฒะฐะฝะฝะฐั ะะณะพ ะะฐะฝ ะขัะตั ะฒ 1926 ะณะพะดั ะฒ ะณะพัะพะดะต ะขัะนะฝะธะฝั ะพะดะฝะพะธะผะตะฝะฝะพะน ะฟัะพะฒะธะฝัะธะธ ะฝะฐ ัะณะต ะัะตัะฝะฐะผะฐ. ะะพะปะฝะพะต ะฝะฐะทะฒะฐะฝะธะต ัะตะปะธะณะธะธ - ยซะะตะปะธะบะธะน ะฟััั ััะตััะตะน ัะฟะพั ะธ ัะฟะฐัะตะฝะธัยป. ะะพะบััะธะฝะฐ ะธ ะบัะปััะพะฒะฐั ะฟัะฐะบัะธะบะฐ ะบะฐะพะดะฐะธะทะผะฐ ัะพะดะตัะถะฐั ัะปะตะผะตะฝัั ัะฐัะฟัะพัััะฐะฝะตะฝะฝัั ะฒะพ ะัะตัะฝะฐะผะต ะฑัะดะดะธะทะผะฐ, ะดะฐะพัะธะทะผะฐ, ะบะพะฝัััะธะฐะฝััะฒะฐ, ะบัะปััะฐ ะฟัะตะดะบะพะฒ ะธ ะบะฐัะพะปะธัะตััะฒะฐ, ะฐ ัะฐะบะถะต ะพัะบัะพะฒะตะฝะธะน, ะฟะพะปััะตะฝะฝัั ะฒ ั ะพะดะต ัะฟะธัะธัะธัะตัะบะธั ัะตะฐะฝัะพะฒ. ะฆะตะปัั ะฒะตััััะธั ััะธัะฐะตััั ะธะทะฑะฐะฒะปะตะฝะธะต ัะตะปะพะฒะตะบะฐ ะพั ัะตะฟะธ ะฟะตัะตัะพะถะดะตะฝะธะน ะธ ัะพะตะดะธะฝะตะฝะธะต ะดััะธ ั ะฑะพะถะตััะฒะพะผ ะะฐะพะดะฐะน. ะัะฐะบัะธะบััััั ะผะตะดะธัะฐัะธะธ, ะพะฑัะตะฝะธะต ั ะดัั ะฐะผะธ ะธ ะดััะณะธะต ะผะธััะธัะตัะบะธะต ะฟัะฐะบัะธะบะธ, ะพั ะดัั ะพะฒะตะฝััะฒะฐ ััะตะฑัะตััั ัะตะปะธะฑะฐั.[PARAGRAPH:2]ะัะณะฐะฝะธะทะฐัะธั ะะฐะพะดะฐะน ะธะณัะฐะปะฐ ะทะฝะฐัะธัะตะปัะฝัั ัะพะปั ะฒ ัะตะณะธะพะฝะฐะปัะฝะพะน ะฟะพะปะธัะธะบะต ะฝะฐ ัะณะต ะัะตัะฝะฐะผะฐ ะฒ ัะตัะตะดะธะฝะต XX ะฒะตะบะฐ, ะพะฑะปะฐะดะฐั, ะฝะตัะผะพััั ะฝะฐ ะดะตะบะปะฐัะธััะตะผัะน ะฟะฐัะธัะธะทะผ ััะตะฝะธั, ัะพะฑััะฒะตะฝะฝัะผะธ ะฒะพะพััะถะตะฝะฝัะผะธ ัะธะปะฐะผะธ ะธ ะบะพะฝััะพะปะธััั ะทะฝะฐัะธัะตะปัะฝัะต ัะตััะธัะพัะธะธ. ะะพ ะฒัะพัะพะน ะฟะพะปะพะฒะธะฝะต XX ะฒะตะบะฐ ะบะฐะพะดะฐะธะทะผ ะฟะพะดะฒะตัะณะฐะปัั ัะตะฟัะตััะธัะผ ะฟะพ ะฟะพะปะธัะธัะตัะบะธะผ ะฟัะธัะธะฝะฐะผ ัะพ ััะพัะพะฝั ัะถะฝะพะฒัะตัะฝะฐะผัะบะพะณะพ ะดะธะบัะฐัะพัะฐ ะะณะพ ะะธะฝั ะัะตะผะฐ, ะฐ ะทะฐัะตะผ - ะฒะปะฐััะตะน ะกะพัะธะฐะปะธััะธัะตัะบะพะน ะ ะตัะฟัะฑะปะธะบะธ ะัะตัะฝะฐะผ ะฟะพ ะฟะพะดะพะทัะตะฝะธั ะฒ ัะฒัะทัั ั ััะฐะฝััะทัะบะธะผะธ ัะฟะตััะปัะถะฑะฐะผะธ.

TEXT.English: The ultimate goal of CaoDaists is to be reunified with the All That Is, to return home.[PARAGRAPH:2]- Hum Dac Bui,[PARAGRAPH:1][SPACE][SPACE]"Cao Dai: Faith of Unity"

รรฐรจรฑรฒรจร รญรฑรฒรขรฎ

TEXT.English: Christianity is the world's largest religion. It is the dominant religion in Europe, North, Central, and South America, and has made significant inroads in all other parts of the world. Christianity is based upon the teachings of Jesus Christ, whom Christians believe to have been the "son of God made flesh," brought to the world to teach men the way of salvation. Jesus lived from approximately 6 BC to 30 AD.[PARAGRAPH:2]Jesus' life and teachings are recorded in the New Testament. Born of the Virgin Mary, a Jew, the Christ lived and taught in and around present-day Palestine and Israel. According to the New Testament, he performed many miracles during his lifetime, and he taught men how to cleanse themselves from sin and thus achieve eternal happiness after death. Jesus was betrayed and crucified, but he rose from death after three days and ascended into heaven.[PARAGRAPH:2]After Christ's death, Christianity spread throughout the eastern Mediterranean. The Romans tried unsuccessfully to suppress it, but eventually they adopted Christianity as the state religion. With the might of Rome behind it, Christianity spread rapidly through the known world. When the Roman Empire fell, Christianity broke into two branches. Since then it has continued to fragment, with a number of the branches quite hostile to others. Today, the major Christian branches include Roman Catholicism, Protestantism, Orthodox Christianity, and Mormonism.
TEXT.French: Le christianisme constitue la religion la plus rรฉpandue dans le monde. Il domine en Europe, en Amรฉrique du Nord et en Amรฉrique latine et s'est largement imposรฉ dans toutes les autres rรฉgions du monde. Le christianisme repose sur les enseignements de Jรฉsus-Christ considรฉrรฉ par les Chrรฉtiens comme le "fils de Dieu fait homme" et mis au monde pour apporter le salut. Jรฉsus a vรฉcu d'environ 6 av. JC ร 30 ap. JC[PARAGRAPH:2]La vie de Jรฉsus et ses enseignements sont consignรฉs dans le Nouveau Testament. Nรฉ d'une mรจre juive, la Vierge Marie, le Christ a vรฉcu et enseignรฉ dans la zone correspondant aujourd'hui ร la Palestine et ร Israรซl. Selon le Nouveau Testament, il a accompli de nombreux miracles au cours de sa vie et a enseignรฉ aux hommes comment se purifier de leurs pรฉchรฉs et parvenir ainsi ร la vie รฉternelle aprรจs la mort. Jรฉsus a รฉtรฉ trahi et crucifiรฉ, mais il est ressuscitรฉ le troisiรจme jour et est montรฉ au ciel[PARAGRAPH:2]Aprรจs la mort du Christ, la chrรฉtientรฉ s'est rรฉpandue dans toute la Mรฉditerranรฉe orientale. Les Romains ont tentรฉ, sans y parvenir, de l'รฉradiquer, avant d'adopter finalement le christianisme comme religion d'Etat. A la chute de l'Empire romain, la chrรฉtientรฉ s'est scindรฉe en deux Eglises et a continuรฉ depuis ร se diviser en diverses branches relativement hostiles les unes envers les autres. Aujourd'hui, les principales Eglises chrรฉtiennes sont le catholicisme romain, le protestantisme, la chrรฉtientรฉ orthodoxe et le mormonisme.
TEXT.German: Das Christentum ist als grรถรte Weltreligion die Hauptreligion Europas, Nord-, Mittel- und Sรผdamerikas. Grundlage des Christentums ist die Lehre Jesus', des "fleischgewordenen Sohns Gottes", der von Maria geboren wurde, um den Menschen die Erlรถsung zu bringen. Jesus lebte um 6 vor bis 30 nach Christus.[PARAGRAPH:2]Sein Leben und Wirken ist im Neuen Testament festgehalten. Jesus lebte und lehrte im heutigen Palรคstina und Israel. Laut des Neuen Testaments vollbrachte er verschiedene Wunder und versprach den Menschen die Vergebung ihrer Sรผnden und die ewige Glรผckseligkeit. Jesus wurde von Judas Ischariot an die Rรถmer ausgeliefert und gekreuzigt. Drei Tage nach seinem Tod stand Jesus von den Toten auf und fuhr in den Himmel auf.[PARAGRAPH:2]Nach Jesus' Tod breitete sich das Christentum im gesamten รถstlichen Mittelmeerraum aus. Die Rรถmer versuchten zunรคchst erfolglos, die neue Religion zu unterdrรผcken, bis schlieรlich Kaiser Konstantin das Christentum zur Staatsreligion erhob. Als das Rรถmische Reich zerfiel, kam es zu einer ersten Spaltung des Christentums. Heute sind der Katholizismus, der Protestantismus sowie das orthodoxe Christentum die wichtigsten Strรถmungen des Christentums.
TEXT.Italian: Il Cristianesimo รจ la religione piรน diffusa del mondo, รจ dominante in Europa e nelle Americhe e si puรฒ trovare anche in comunitร in altre parti del mondo. Il Cristianesimo si basa sugli insegnamenti di Gesรน Cristo, che i Cristiani credono sia stato "il figlio di Dio sceso in Terra", venuto al mondo per insegnare agli uomini la via della salvezza. Gesรน visse approssimativamente tra il 6 a. C e il 30 d. C.[PARAGRAPH:2]La vita di Gesรน e i suoi insegnamenti sono raccolti nel Nuovo Testamento: figlio della Vergine Maria e di un Ebreo, Cristo visse e insegnรฒ nella zona tra Palestina e Israele. Stando al Nuovo Testamento, durante la sua vita compรฌ molti miracoli e insegnรฒ agli uomini come liberarsi dai propri peccati e come raggiungere la pace eterna dopo la morte. Gesรน venne tradito e crocefisso, ma risorse dalla morte dopo tre giorni e salรฌ in Paradiso.[PARAGRAPH:2]Dopo la morte di Cristo, il Cristianesimo si diffuse nella zona orientale del Mediterraneo: i Romani tentarono di sopprimerlo, ma successivamente adottarono il Cristianesimo come religione ufficiale; grazie alla potenza di Roma, la nuova religione si diffuse rapidamente nel mondo conosciuto. Con la caduta dell'Impero Romano, il Cristianesimo si divise in due gruppi: da allora ha continuato a frammentarsi, addirittura con la creazione di fazioni molto ostili tra loro. Oggi, i gruppi principali sono il Cattolicesimo Romano, il Protestantesimo, il Cristianesimo Ortodosso e il Mormonismo.
TEXT.Spanish: El cristianismo es la religiรณn mรกs extendida del mundo. Predomina en Europa y Amรฉrica y tiene una importante presencia en las demรกs zonas del mundo. Se basa en las enseรฑanzas de Jesucristo, "el hijo de Dios hecho carne" para los cristianos, venido al mundo para mostrar a los hombres el camino de la salvaciรณn. Jesรบs viviรณ aproximadamente del aรฑo 6 a. C. al aรฑo 30 d. C.[PARAGRAPH:2]Su vida y sus enseรฑanzas se narran en el Nuevo Testamento. Nacido de la virgen Marรญa, una judรญa, Cristo viviรณ y enseรฑรณ en la zona donde hoy en dรญa se encuentran Palestina e Israel. Segรบn el Nuevo Testamento, durante su vida realizรณ numerosos milagros y enseรฑรณ a los hombres a purificarse del pecado y alcanzar la felicidad eterna tras la muerte. Jesรบs fue traicionado y crucificado, pero se alzรณ de entre los muertos tres dรญas despuรฉs y ascendiรณ a los cielos.[PARAGRAPH:2]Tras su muerte, el cristianismo se propagรณ rรกpidamente por el Mediterrรกneo oriental. Los romanos trataron de reprimirlo sin mucho รฉxito, hasta el punto de que acabarรญan adoptรกndolo como religiรณn de estado. Respaldado por el poder de Roma, se difundiรณ rรกpidamente por todo el orbe. Tras la caรญda del Imperio Romano, el cristianismo se dividiรณ en dos ramas. Desde entonces esa fragmentaciรณn se ha incrementado, y la hostilidad entre las diferentes facciones ha alcanzado cotas muy elevadas. En la actualidad, las ramas mรกs importantes del cristianismo son el catolicismo romano, el protestantismo, el cristianismo ortodoxo y el mormonismo.

รรฎรญรดรณรถรจร รญรฑรฒรขรฎ

TEXT.English: Confucianism is an East Asian religion based upon the teachings of Confucius. Confucius (known in Chinese as Kong Fu Zi) lived between 552 and 479 BC. Confucius lived in China during an especially turbulent period, when the country lacked central leadership and various small states fought constantly for dominance. As much a philosopher and political theorist as a religious leader, Confucius sought to teach others the correct behavior for man in society. His philosophy emphasized personal and governmental morality and strong family loyalty. He believed that each man had a place in society, and that each should behave righteously and correctly according to that place. This included the ruler, who needed to be devoted to the people rather than to personal aggrandizement.[PARAGRAPH:2]Confucianism grew in influence for the two centuries following Confucius' death, until it was rigidly suppressed during the Qin Dynasty, whose leaders saw it as a threat to their power. The Qin systematically destroyed the Confucian temples and burned all the Confucian books, including most of Confucius' writings. Virtually everything was lost, except for a cache of important books hidden in the walls of a believer's home. When these were discovered, the few remaining believers were able to rebuild and spread their religion. Eventually Confucianism became the state religion of China. It remained so until the Communists came into power in the 20th Century.[PARAGRAPH:2]Today, Confucianism is enjoying something of a revival, and many Chinese students (and students from around the world) study the great philosopher and his teachings. However, in modern times Confucianism is mostly studied as a philosophy rather than a religion (though it should be pointed out that there is no clear distinction between the two in Eastern thought).
TEXT.French: Le confucianisme constitue une religion d'Asie du Sud-Est fondรฉe sur les enseignements de Confucius (552 ร 479 av. JC). Confucius (ou Kong Fu Zi en chinois) a vรฉcu en Chine durant une pรฉriode particuliรจrement agitรฉe, ร l'heure oรน le pays avait cruellement besoin d'un pouvoir central fort et oรน divers petits Etats se livraient ร une lutte incessante. Tout autant philosophe et thรฉoricien politique que chef religieux, Confucius cherchait ร enseigner le comportement correct de l'homme en sociรฉtรฉ. Sa philosophie accordait une grande importance ร la moralitรฉ personnelle et gouvernementale ainsi qu'ร une loyautรฉ familiale forte. Il considรฉrait que tout homme avait une place dans la sociรฉtรฉ et qu'il devait se comporter de faรงon vertueuse et biensรฉante en fonction de cette place. Ceci valait รฉgalement pour le dirigeant, qui devait รชtre dรฉvouรฉ au peuple et ne pas privilรฉgier son avancement personnel.[PARAGRAPH:2]Le confucianisme a vu son influence grandir au cours des deux siรจcles ayant suivi la mort de Confucius, avant d'รชtre complรจtement supprimรฉ sous la dynastie Qin: les dirigeants le considรฉraient en effet comme une menace pour leur pouvoir. Les Qin ont systรฉmatiquement dรฉtruit les temples confucianistes et brรปlรฉ tous les livres sur le confucianisme, y compris la majoritรฉ des รฉcrits de Confucius. Pratiquement tout a disparu, exceptรฉ des livres importants cachรฉs dans les murs de la maison d'un fidรจle. A leur dรฉcouverte, les quelques ultimes adeptes ont pu rรฉtablir leur religion et la rรฉpandre. Finalement, le confucianisme est devenu la religion d'Etat en Chine et l'est restรฉe jusqu'ร l'arrivรฉe au pouvoir des Communistes au XXe siรจcle.[PARAGRAPH:2]Aujourd'hui, le confucianisme bรฉnรฉficie d'une sorte de renouveau et de nombreux รฉtudiants chinois (et du monde entier) รฉtudient le grand philosophe et ses enseignements. A l'heure actuelle, il est cependant davantage รฉtudiรฉ comme philosophie que comme religion, bien que la distinction ne soit pas nette dans la pensรฉe orientale.
TEXT.German: Der Konfuzianismus ist eine ostasiatische Religion, die auf den Lehren Konfuzius' basiert. Konfuzius (Chinesisch: "Kong Fu Zi") lebte um 552 bis 479 vor Christus in China, als unzรคhlige Feudalherren um die Vorherrschaft kรคmpften. Konfuzius war Philosoph, Theoretiker und Glaubensstifter. Er war entschlossen, seine Mitmenschen das richtige Verhalten innerhalb der Gesellschaft zu lehren. Seine Philosophie umfasste die persรถnliche und die Regierungsmoral sowie die Bindungen innerhalb der Familie. In seinen Augen hatte jeder Mensch einen festen Platz innerhalb der Gesellschaft und musste sich seiner gesellschaftlichen Stellung entsprechend verhalten. Dies galt auch fรผr den Herrscher, der laut Konfuzius in erster Linie seinem Volk Rechenschaft schuldig war.[PARAGRAPH:2]Der Konfuzianismus gewann in den Jahrhunderten nach Konfuzius' Tod an Einfluss, bis er wรคhrend der Qin-Dynastie unterdrรผckt wurde. Die Qin-Herrscher zerstรถrten systematisch alle konfuzianischen Tempel und verbrannten nahezu alle konfuzianischen Schriften. Mit Ausnahme einiger wichtiger (in den Mauern des Hauses eines Anhรคngers der konfuzianischen Lehre versteckter) Bรผcher wurden alle Schriften vernichtet. Dank dieser Bรผcher konnten die wenigen รผberlebenden Anhรคnger ihre Religion erneuern und erneut verbreiten. Spรคter war der Konfuzianismus in China (bis zur Machtergreifung der Kommunisten) sogar Staatsreligion.[PARAGRAPH:2]Heute erlebt der Konfuzianismus eine kleine Renaissance. Viele (nicht nur chinesische) Studenten studieren den groรen Philosophen und seine Lehren. Allerdings gilt Konfuzius heute eher als Philosoph denn als Religionsstifter.
TEXT.Italian: Il Confucianesimo รจ una religione dell'Est Asiatico, fondata sugli insegnamenti di Confucio (in Cinese Kong Fu Zi), che visse in Cina tra il 552 e il 479 a. C., in un periodo turbolento nel quale la nazione era priva di un governo centrale, mentre altri piccoli stati lottavano tra loro per il dominio del territorio. Tanto filosofo politico quanto leader religioso, Confucio cercรฒ di insegnare il corretto comportamento di un uomo all'interno della societร : la sua filosofia enfatizzava l'etica morale di ogni individuo e del governo, oltre a forti legami famigliari. Confucio credeva che ogni uomo avesse un posto nella societร e ciascuno dovesse agire giustamente e correttamente a seconda del suo ruolo; il discorso valeva anche per il sovrano, che doveva essere devoto verso il suo popolo.[PARAGRAPH:2]Il Confucianesimo diventรฒ molto influente nei due secoli successivi alla morte di Confucio, finchรฉ non venne soppresso durante la dinastia Qin, i cui leader credevano potesse rappresentare un pericolo per la conservazione del potere. I Qin distrussero sistematicamente ogni tempio e ogni libro di Confucio, comprese la maggior parte delle sue scritture. Venne distrutto praticamente tutto, tranne alcuni importanti libri, nascosti nelle mura della casa di un fedele. Quando vennero scoperti, i seguaci rimanenti furono in grado di ricostruire e diffondere nuovamente la loro religione. In seguito, il Confucianesimo divenne la religione ufficiale della Cina ed รจ rimasta tale fino all'avvento del regime comunista, nel XX secolo.[PARAGRAPH:2]Al giorno d'oggi, il Confucianesimo sta vivendo un periodo di rinascita e molti studenti Cinesi (ma anche di tutto il mondo), studiano il grande filosofo e i suoi insegnamenti. Tuttavia, oggigiorno il Confucianesimo viene studiato piรน come filosofia, piuttosto che come religione (malgrado non esista una netta distinzione tra le due cose, almeno in Oriente).
TEXT.Spanish: El confucianismo es una religiรณn del Lejano Oriente basada en las enseรฑanzas de Confucio, (conocido en chino como Kong Fu Zi), que viviรณ entre el 552 y el 479 a. C. En aquel tiempo, China vivรญa un periodo especialmente turbulento, sin un liderazgo central y disputada por varios estados menores. Confucio, filรณsofo y teรณrico polรญtico ademรกs de lรญder religioso, incluyรณ entre sus enseรฑanzas un importante componente รฉtico. Su filosofรญa subrayaba especialmente la moralidad personal y gubernamental, asรญ como la importancia de los lazos familiares. Para รฉl, cada hombre tenรญa un lugar en la sociedad, y debรญa comportarse con rectitud y correcciรณn en funciรณn de ese lugar. Esto tambiรฉn se aplicaba al gobernante, quien debรญa actuar por el bien del pueblo, y no por el suyo propio.[PARAGRAPH:2]La influencia del confucianismo fue aumentando durante los dos siglos posteriores a la muerte de su creador, hasta que fue severamente reprimido por la dinastรญa Qin, cuyos lรญderes lo consideraban una amenaza para su poder. Los Qin destruyeron sistemรกticamente los templos confucionistas y quemaron todos sus libros, incluida la mayor parte de las escrituras del propio Confucio. Todo se perdiรณ, salvo algunos libros importantes que quedaron escondidos en la pared de la casa de uno de sus seguidores. Cuando fueron descubiertos, los fieles supervivientes pudieron reconstruir y difundir de nuevo su religiรณn. Con el tiempo, el confucianismo acabรณ por convertirse en la religiรณn oficial en China. Siguiรณ siรฉndolo hasta que los comunistas se hicieron con el poder en el siglo XX.[PARAGRAPH:2]En la actualidad, el confucianismo vive una especie de renacimiento, y muchos chinos (y gente de todo el mundo) estudian al gran filรณsofo y sus enseรฑanzas. No obstante, se estudia mรกs como una filosofรญa que como una religiรณn (aunque debe seรฑalarse que en Oriente no hay una distinciรณn clara entre ambos conceptos).

TEXT.English: "Go before the people with your example, and be laborious in their affairs." - Confucius
TEXT.French: "Montre au peuple l'exemple, et soyez laborieux dans leurs affaires." - Confucius
TEXT.Spanish: "Predica con el ejemplo ante tu gente, y sรฉ laborioso en cuanto a sus asuntos." - Confucio
TEXT.Polish: "IdŸ przed luŸmi ze swoim przyk³adem, i pracowity dla ich spraw." - Konfucjusz
TEXT.Russian: ยซะะดะธ ะฟะพะทะฐะดะธ ะปัะดะตะน ัะพ ัะฒะพะธะผ ะฟัะธะผะตัะพะผ ะธ ะฑัะดั ัััะดะพะปัะฑะธะฒ ะฒ ะธั ะดะตะปะฐั ยป. - ะะพะฝัััะธะน

TEXT.English: A druid was a member of the high-ranking professional class in ancient Celtic cultures.[PARAGRAPH:2]While perhaps best remembered as religious leaders, they were also legal authorities, adjudicators, lorekeepers, medical professionals, and political advisors. While the druids are reported to have been literate, they are believed to have been prevented by doctrine from recording their knowledge in written form, thus they left no written accounts of themselves. They are however attested in some detail by their contemporaries from other cultures, such as the Romans and the Greeks.[PARAGRAPH:2]Druidic lore consisted of a large number of verses learned by heart, and Caesar remarked that it could take up to twenty years to complete the course of study. What was taught to druid novices anywhere is conjecture: of the druids' oral literature, not one certifiably ancient verse is known to have survived, even in translation. All instruction was communicated orally, but for ordinary purposes, Caesar reports, the Gauls had a written language in which they used Greek characters. In this he probably draws on earlier writers; by the time of Caesar, Gaulish inscriptions had moved from the Greek script to the Latin script.[PARAGRAPH:2]Greek and Roman writers frequently made reference to the druids as practitioners of human sacrifice. According to Caesar, those who had been found guilty of theft or other criminal offences were considered preferable for use as sacrificial victims, but when criminals were in short supply, innocents would be acceptable. A form of sacrifice recorded by Caesar was the burning alive of victims in a large wooden effigy, now often known as a wicker man. A differing account came from the 10th-century Commenta Bernensia, which claimed that sacrifices to the deities Teutates, Esus and Taranis were by drowning, hanging and burning, respectively.[PARAGRAPH:2]Diodorus Siculus, writing in 36 BCE, described how the druids followed "the Pythagorean doctrine", that human souls "are immortal and after a prescribed number of years they commence a new life in a new body." In 1928, folklorist Donald A. Mackenzie speculated that Buddhist missionaries had been sent by the Indian king Ashoka. Others have invoked common Indo-European parallels. Caesar noted the druidic doctrine of the original ancestor of the tribe, whom he referred to as Dispater, or Father Hades.[PARAGRAPH:2]Druids also play a prominent role in Irish Folklore, generally serving lords and kings as high ranking priest-counselors with the gift of prophecy and other assorted mystical abilities - the best example of these possibly being Cathbad. The chief druid in the court of King Conchobar mac Nessa of Ulster, Cathbad features in several tales, most of which detail his ability to foretell the future. In the tale of Deirdre of the Sorrows โ the foremost tragic heroine of the Ulster Cycle โ the druid prophesied before the court of Conchobar that Deirdre would grow up to be very beautiful, but that kings and lords would go to war over her, much blood would be shed because of her, and Ulster's three greatest warriors would be forced into exile for her sake. This prophecy, ignored by the king, came true.
TEXT.German: Ein Druide war ein Mitglied der priesterlichen und gelehrten Klasse in der antiken keltischen Gesellschaft in Westeuropa, Britanien und Irland. Sie wurden durch die romische Regierung unterdrรผckt und verschwinden aus den schriftlichen รberlieferungen im zweiten Jahrhundert nach Christi. Druiden verbanden die Aufgaben von Priestern, Richtern, Gelehrten und Lehrern. Es gibt wenige zeitgenรถssische Hinweise auf ihre Existenz, so dass wir heute wenig mit Sicherheit รผber sie sagen kรถnnen, jedoch spielen sie eine wichtige Rolle in den spรคteren irischen Mythen und Literatur.[PARAGRAPH:2]Als die frรผheste Aufzeichnung des Namens druidae werden die verlorenen Werke des griechischen Doxographen Sotion von Alexandrien (frรผhes zweites Jahrhundert vor Christi) bezeichnet, der im dritten Jahrhundert nach Christus von Diogenes Laertius zitiert wurde.[PARAGRAPH:2]Die keltischen Gemeinschaften, in denen Druiden dienten, waren polytheistisch. Sie zeigten auch Merkmale von Animismus durch ihre Verehrung verschiedener Aspekte der Natur, so wie Land, Meer und Himmel, sowie durch die Ehrfurcht vor anderen Naturaspekten, wie heilige Bรคume und Haine, (Eiche und Haselnussstrauch wurden besonders verehrt), Hรผgelspitzen, Wasserlรคufen, Seen und Pflanzen, wie zum Beispiel die Mistel. Feuer wurde als das Symbol verschiedener Gรถtter angesehen und wurde mit Reinigung assoziert. Angebliche rituelle Tรถtungen und Menschenopfer waren Aspekte der druidischen Kultur, die klassische Schriftsteller schockierten.
TEXT.Italian: Un druido era un membro della classe sacerdotale e istruita nelle antiche societร celtiche dell'Europa occidentale, della Gran Bretagna e dell'Irlanda. Furono soppressi dal governo romano e scomparvero dalla storia intorno al II secolo d.C. avevano le funzioni di sacerdoti, giudici, studiosi e insegnanti. Poche prove esistono di loro attualmente, e quindi poco si puรฒ dire di loro con certezza, ma continuarono a vivere nel mito e nella letteratura irlandese seguente.[PARAGRAPH:2]La prima testimonianza del nome druidae รจ segnalato da un'opera perduta dello scrittore greco Sozione di Alessandria (inizi del II secolo a.C.), che รจ stato citato da Diogene Laerzio nel III secolo d.C.[PARAGRAPH:2]Le comunitร celtiche che i druidi servivano erano politeiste; mostravano anche segni di animismo, nel loro rispetto per i vari aspetti del mondo naturale, come la terra, il mare e il cielo, e del loro culto di altri aspetti della natura, come alberi e boschetti sacri (la quercia e il nocciolo erano particolarmente venerati), cime di colline, torrenti, laghi e piante come il vischio. Il fuoco era ritenuto un simbolo di diverse divinitร ed รจ stato associato con la pulizia. Le uccisioni rituali e i sacrifici umani erano gli aspetti della cultura druidica che sconvolsero gli scrittori classici.
TEXT.Polish: Druid (³ac. druides albo druida, z celtyckiego) - staro¿ytny kap³an celtycki, który przewodzi³ obrzêdom i ceremoniom religijnym. Druidzi sprawowali tak¿e s¹dy oraz przepowiadali przysz³oœæ z lotu ptaków lub z wnêtrznoœci zwierz¹t. Jedna z trzech kast kap³añskich, obok bardów i filidów (vates), u Celtów.

ัะทััะตัะบะพะน ัะตะปะธะณะธะธ, ะฝะฐัะฐะฒะฝะต ั ะฟะพะบะปะพะฝะตะฝะธะตะผ ะฑะพะณะฐะผ ะธ ะฝะธะทัะธะผ ะดัั ะฐะผ. ะ ะตะปะธะณะธะพะทะฝัะต ะฒะตัะพะฒะฐะฝะธั ะธ ะฟัะฐะบัะธะบะธ ะบะตะปััะพะฒ ะฟัะตัััะฟะฝะธะบะธ, ะฐ ะฟัะธ ะพััััััะฒะธะธ ัะฐะบะพะฒัั - ะธ ะฝะตะฒะธะฝะฝัะต ะปัะดะธ. ะะปะธะฝะธะน ะกัะฐััะธะน ะพะฟะธััะฒะฐะป ะบะฐะฝะฝะธะฑะฐะปะธะทะผ ะดััะธะดะพะฒ - ัะพ ะตััั Alveston) ะฝะฐ ัะณะต ะณัะฐัััะฒะฐ ะะปะพััะตััะธั, ะฐ ัะฐะบะถะต ะฒ ัะพัััะฝะพะผ ะฑะพะปะพัะต ะะธะฝะดะพั-ะผะพัั ะฑะปะธะท ะดะตัะตะฒะฝะธ ะะพะฑะฑะตัะปะธ, ะณัะฐัััะฒะพ ะงะตัะธั, ะะตะปะธะบะพะฑัะธัะฐะฝะธั (ั. ะฝ. ยซัะตะปะพะฒะตะบ ะธะท ะะธะฝะดะพัยป) - ะฟะพะดัะฒะตัะถะดะฐัั ัะพะพะฑัะตะฝะธั ัะธะผะปัะฝ. ะขะฐะบ, ะฒ ะฟะตัะตัะต ะฒ ะะปะฒะตััะพะฝะต ะฝะฐะนะดะตะฝั ะบะพััะธ ะฟัะธะผะตัะฝะพ 150 ัะตะปะพะฒะตะบ, ะฒ ัะพะผ ัะธัะปะต - ะถะตะฝัะธะฝ, ัะฑะธััั , ะบะฐะบ ััะธัะฐัั ะฐัั ะตะพะปะพะณะธ, ะฒ ะถะตััะฒะตะฝะฝัั ัะตะปัั . ะะฐั ะพะดะบะฐ ะฒ ะะปะฒะตััะพะฝะต ะดะฐัะธััะตััั ะฟัะธะผะตัะฝะพ ัะตัะตะดะธะฝะพะน I ะฒะตะบะฐ ะฝ.ั. - ัะพ ะตััั ะธะผะตะฝะฝะพ ัะพะณะดะฐ, ะบะพะณะดะฐ ัะธะผะปัะฝะต ะฐะบัะธะฒะฝะพ ะทะฐะฒะพะตะฒัะฒะฐะปะธ ะัะธัะฐะฝัะบะธะต ะพัััะพะฒะฐ. ะััั ะผะฝะตะฝะธะต, ััะพ ัะธะผะปัะฝะต ัะฝะธััะพะถะฐะปะธ ะดััะธะดะพะฒ ะบะฐะบ ะฒะดะพั ะฝะพะฒะธัะตะปะตะน ะธ ะพัะณะฐะฝะธะทะฐัะพัะพะฒ ะบะตะปัััะบะพะณะพ ัะพะฟัะพัะธะฒะปะตะฝะธั. ะะพะทะผะพะถะฝะพ, ะพะฟะธัะฐะฝะฝัะต ะฒััะต ะถะตััะฒั ะฑัะปะธ ะฟัะธะฝะตัะตะฝั, ััะพะฑั ะทะฐัััะธัััั ะฟะพะดะดะตัะถะบะพะน ะฑะพะณะพะฒ ะฒ ะฒะพะนะฝะต ะฟัะพัะธะฒ ัะธะผะปัะฝ. ะะฐะบ ัะฐะท ะฒ ััะพ ะฒัะตะผั (40 - 60 ะณะณ. ะฝ. ั.) ัะธะผัะบะธะต ะฒะพะนัะบะฐ ะฟะพะด ััะบะพะฒะพะดััะฒะพะผ ัะฝะฐัะฐะปะฐ ะฑัะดััะตะณะพ ะธะผะฟะตัะฐัะพัะฐ ะะตัะฟะฐัะธะฐะฝะฐ, ะฐ ะฟะพัะพะผ - ะฝะฐะผะตััะฝะธะบะฐ ะะฐั ะกะฒะตัะพะฝะธั ะะฐัะปะธะฝะฐ ะฐะบัะธะฒะฝะพ ะฟัะพะดะฒะธะณะฐะปะธัั ะฒะณะปัะฑั ะัะธัะฐะฝะธะธ. ะะดะฝะฐะบะพ ะถะตััะฒั ะฝะต ะฟะพะผะพะณะปะธ: ะฒะพ ะฒัะตะผั ะฒะพะนะฝั ั ะะพัะดะธะบะบะพะน ะฒ 60 ะณะพะดั ะฝ. ั. ัะธะผัะบะธะต ะฒะพะนัะบะฐ ะทะฐั ะฒะฐัะธะปะธ ะณะปะฐะฒะฝัะน ะพะฟะปะพั ะฑัะธัะฐะฝัะบะธั ะดััะธะดะพะฒ - TEXT.English: So, apart from casting runes, what other hobbies do you have - forbidden rituals... human sacrifices?[PARAGRAPH:2]- Simona Panova,[PARAGRAPH:1][SPACE][SPACE]"Nightmarish Sacrifice"
TEXT.French: "Les Gaulois ont des hommes sages, experts des Dieux, qu'ils nomment Druides. A travers les entrailles et les sacrifices d'animaux, ils peuvent prรฉduire le futur, et personne n'ose douter de leur parole. Dans ce type de divination provenant de la Nature, ces รฉclairรฉs accordent la plus haute importance dans la tradition antique de l'observation." - Diodorus
TEXT.Polish: "Galowie mieli specjalnych kap³anów i mêdrców zajmuj¹cych siê kontaktem z bogami. To byli Druidzi." - Diodor

TEXT.English: The Hellenic religion, also referred to as Greek mythology, comprises an enormous body of stories about gods, goddesses, and heroes. These stories date back many centuries, preserved in pre-literate Greece through oral tradition. The Hellenic religion was polytheistic in nature, with innumerable local nature deities in addition to the major Olympian gods and goddesses. The Greek gods were human in form but possessed supernatural powers; they were born from other gods but never aged, they were immortal, could travel enormous distances instantly, speak through humans with or without their knowledge, etc. Some gods were the personified aspects of natural forces (for example, Helios was literally the god of the sun) while others had complex personalities and multiple functions associated with them (Apollo is probably the best example).[PARAGRAPH:1]The gods and goddesses were all related to one another as part of one large family, but there were clear generations of gods, and their interactions with each other were sometimes violent. For example, the Olympian gods had reached their place of dominance by casting out an earlier generation of gods and goddesses known as the Titans. This may be a mythical reflection of the struggle between the Greeks and the earlier culture that they displaced when arriving in the Peloponnesus around roughly 1000 BC.[PARAGRAPH:1]In addition to stories about gods, the Hellenic religion was also full of great sagas about the deeds of heroes, many of whom possessed a divine parent. Stories abound about figures such as Heracles, Perseus, Jason, and the characters of the Iliad. These humans frequently interacted with gods and even bested them at times. Many of the heroes were held in great esteem by the Greeks, especially "noble" characters such as Achilles.[PARAGRAPH:1]Although some of the well-educated Greeks had their doubts about the existence of the gods, the vast majority of Greeks did not question the validity of the stories. The Greeks used their religion to explain the doings of the natural world and their own origins; it was a source of pride to trace one's lineage or the founding of one's city to the actions of a god.[PARAGRAPH:1]Archaeology and linguistic analysis suggests that the Hellenic religion was heavily influenced by other religions. For example, the Greek Zeus, Roman Jupiter, Germanic Tyr, and Sanskrit Dyaus are all sky gods that share a common Indo-European origin sometime in the distant past. Other gods were borrowed or influenced from Near Eastern religions; Aphrodite possessed a very similar function to the Semitic goddesses Ishtar and Astarte. During the Hellenic period and afterwards, the Greek religion spread across much of the Mediterranean and Near East, influencing many of the previously existing religions and giving rise to new syncretistic mystery cults. Although the Hellenic religion is no longer followed, it continues to provide inspiration for artists throughout the world.
TEXT.French: La religion hellรฉnique, ร laquelle on fait le plus souvent rรฉfรฉrence sous l'appellation de mythologie grecque, comprend un grand nombre de lรฉgendes sur les dieux, dรฉesses et hรฉros. Certaines de ces lรฉgendes remontent ร plusieurs siรจcles, ayant รฉtรฉ transmises par la tradition orale. La religion hellรฉnique est polythรฉiste, avec d'innombrables divinitรฉs locales de la nature en plus des principaux dieux du panthรฉon olympien. Les dieux grecs ont forme humaine mais possรจdent des pouvoirs surnaturels, ont รฉtรฉ engendrรฉs par d'autres dieux mais ne vieillissent pas, ils sont immortels, peuvent voyager sur de trรจs grandes distances instantanรฉment, parler ร travers les humains (parfois sans mรชme que ceux-ci s'en aperรงoivent), etc. Certains dieux sont les personnifications des forces de la nature (Hรฉlios รฉtant littรฉralement le dieu du soleil) alors que d'autres ont des personnalitรฉs complexes et sont associรฉs ร de multiples fonctions (Apollon en รฉtant le meilleur exemple).[PARAGRAPH:1]Dieux et dรฉesses sont tous parents d'une mรชme grande famille, mais il existe des gรฉnรฉrations distinctes de dieux, et leurs interactions sont parfois trรจs violentes. Le fait est que les dieux olympiens n'occupent leur position dominante qu'aprรจs avoir chassรฉ une premiรจre gรฉnรฉration de dieux appelรฉs les Titans. Cela reflรจte sans doute le chevauchement culturel mythologique existant entre les Grecs et la civilisation prรฉcรฉdente, chassรฉe ร leur arrivรฉe dans le Pรฉloponnรจse aux alentours de 1000 av. JC.[PARAGRAPH:1]Outre de nombreuses lรฉgendes sur les dieux, la tradition orale hellรฉnique est riche de grandes sagas relatant les hauts faits de hรฉros humains, dont un certain nombre ont une ascendance divine. On pense notamment aux lรฉgendes d'Hรฉraclรจs, de Persรฉe, de Jason et des personnages de l'Iliade. Ces mortels entrent souvent en conflit avec des dieux et en triomphent parfois. Nombre d'entre eux, surtout s'ils sont nobles et puissants comme Achille, sont tenus en haute estime par les Grecs.[PARAGRAPH:1]Mรชme si certains des Grecs รฉduquรฉs entretiennent quelques doutes ร propos de la rรฉalitรฉ dessinรฉe par la religion hellรฉnique, la grande majoritรฉ de la population accepte sans rรฉserve la mythologie comme seule vรฉritรฉ. Les Grecs s'appuient sur leur religion pour expliquer les phรฉnomรจnes naturels et leurs propres origines, et c'est une source de grande fiertรฉ que de pouvoir associer une lignรฉe ou la fondation d'une citรฉ aux actions d'un dieu.[PARAGRAPH:1]Les rรฉsultats des recherches archรฉologiques et linguistiques laissent penser que la religion hellรฉnique a รฉtรฉ trรจs influencรฉe par d'autres religions. C'est ainsi que le dieu grec Zeus, le dieu romain Jupiter, le dieu germain Tyr et le dieu sanskrit Dyaus sont tous des dieux du ciel, et il est probable qu'ils partagent tous une mรชme lointaine origine indo-europรฉenne. Les Grecs empruntent librement des dieux ร d'autres cultes du Proche-Orient, c'est ainsi qu'Aphrodite a des fonctions trรจs proches de celles des dรฉesses sรฉmites Ishtar et Astartรฉ. Au cours de l'รฉpoque hellรฉnique et par la suite, la religion grecque se rรฉpand ร travers la plus grande partie de la Mรฉditerranรฉe et du Proche-Orient, influenรงant nombre des cultes dรฉjร existants et donnant naissance ร de nouveaux cultes syncrรฉtistes. Bien que la religion grecque ne soit plus pratiquรฉe de nos jours, ses mythologies continuent d'enflammer les imaginations et d'illuminer les esprits.
TEXT.German: Die hellenistische Religion der Antike, auch als griechische Mythologie bezeichnet, umfasste einen enormen Fundus an Gรถtter- und Heldensagen. Diese Geschichten datieren viele Jahrhunderte zurรผck und blieben in der prรคliterarischen Zeit durch mรผndliche รberlieferung erhalten. Der hellenistische Glaube war angesichts der vielen Gรถtter und Gรถttinnen des Olymps sowie zahlloser regionaler Naturgottheiten extrem polytheistisch geprรคgt. Die griechischen Gรถtter wurden in der Regel in menschlicher Form dargestellt, besaรen allerdings รผbernatรผrliche Krรคfte. Sie wurden von anderen Gottheiten geboren, alterten nicht, waren unsterblich, konnten im Nu enorme Entfernungen รผberwinden, sprachen durch die Menschen (mit oder ohne deren Wissen) etc. Einige Gรถtter verkรถrperten Naturgewalten (zum Beispiel war Helios im wรถrtlichen Sinne der Gott der Sonne), wรคhrend andere komplexe Persรถnlichkeitsstrukturen aufwiesen und zahlreiche Funktionen wahrnahmen (zum Beispiel Apollon).[PARAGRAPH:1]Als Teil einer groรen Familie waren die Gรถtter und Gรถttinnen alle irgendwie miteinander verwandt, aber es gab eine klare Generationeneinteilung und manchmal bekรคmpften sie sich auch gegenseitig. Zum Beispiel sollen die Gรถtter des Olymps ihre Vorherrschaft durch den Sturz der vorangehenden Gรถttergeneration, der so genannten Titanen, gesichert haben. Hierbei kรถnnte es sich um eine mythische Darstellung der kulturellen รberschneidung zwischen den Griechen und der frรผheren Population in dieser Region handeln, die sie bei der Ankunft auf dem Peleponnes um 1000 vor Christus vertrieben hatten.[PARAGRAPH:1]Neben den vielen Gรถttergeschichten wurde die griechische Religion auch von zahlreichen grandiosen Sagen รผber die Abenteuer verschiedener Helden beeinflusst, von denen viele zumindest ein gรถttliches Elternteil besaรen. Die Erzรคhlungen handelten von heldenhaften Persรถnlichkeiten wie Herakles, Perseus, Jason sowie den Charakteren der Ilias und inspirierten viele griechische Kรผnstler spรคterer Generationen. Diese Helden in Menschengestalt kamen hรคufig mit den Gรถttern in Kontakt, รผbertrumpften sie bisweilen sogar und wurden von den Griechen meist hoch geschรคtzt, insbesondere so ehrenhafte Persรถnlichkeiten wie zum Beispiel Achilles.[PARAGRAPH:1]Einige der griechischen Gelehrten hatten durchaus Zweifel an dem Wahrheitsgehalt der griechischen Mythologie, dennoch stellte der รผberwiegende Teil der Bevรถlkerung die Stichhaltigkeit der รผberlieferten Legenden nicht in Frage. Die Griechen nutzten ihre Religion, um natรผrliche Vorgรคnge und auch ihren eigenen Ursprung zu erklรคren. Man war stolz darauf, wenn man seine eigene Herkunft oder die Grรผndung seiner Heimatstadt auf das Einwirken eines Gottes zurรผckfรผhren konnte.[PARAGRAPH:1]Archรคologische und sprachliche Untersuchungen haben ergeben, dass die griechische Religion stark von anderen Religionen beeinflusst war. Zum Beispiel stellen der griechische Gott Zeus, der rรถmische Gott Jupiter, der germanische Gott Tyr und der Sanskrit-Gott Dyaus allesamt Himmelsgรถtter dar, die irgendwann in der fernen Vergangenheit einen gemeinsamen indo-europรคischen Ursprung hatten. Andere Gรถtter wurden von den Religionen im Nahen Osten "ausgeborgt" oder zumindest beeinflusst: Aphrodite besaร frappierend รคhnliche Eigenschaften und Funktionen wie die semitischen Gรถttinnen Ishtar und Astarte. Wรคhrend der hellenischen Periode und auch danach kam es zu einer starken Verbreitung der griechischen Mythologie รผber groรe Teile des Mittelmeerraums und des Nahen Ostens. Sie beeinflusste viele der bereits existierenden Religionen und ebnete den Weg fรผr die Entstehung neuer synkretistischer Mysterienkulte. Obwohl die griechische Religion des hellenistischen Zeitalters heute nicht mehr in dieser Form praktiziert wird, lassen sich auch heute noch Kรผnstler in aller Welt von ihr inspirieren.
TEXT.Italian: La religione ellenica, nota anche come "mitologia greca", รจ composta da un'enorme serie di storie di dei, dee ed eroi, tramandate a lungo tempo oralmente nell'era preletteraria. La religione ellenica era politeistica e oltre agli dei e alle dee dell'Olimpo comprendeva innumerevoli divinitร naturali locali. Le divinitร greche avevano aspetto umano ma erano dotate di poteri soprannaturali; pur nascendo (da altre divinitร ) non invecchiavano ed erano immortali, potevano coprire enormi distanze istantaneamente, parlare attraverso gli uomini con o senza la loro consapevolezza, ecc. Alcune divinitร erano la personificazione di forze naturali (per esempio, Elio era letteralmente il dio del sole), mentre altre avevano personalitร complesse e numerose funzioni (Apollo ne รจ forse il miglior esempio).[PARAGRAPH:1]Le divinitร avevano fra loro vincoli di parentela e formavano una grande famiglia, ma potevano distinguersi diverse generazioni, le cui interazioni con le altre avevano spesso esiti violenti. Per fare un esempio, gli dei olimpici avevano raggiunto la loro posizione di dominio estromettendo la precedente generazione dei Titani. Questo fenomeno potrebbe essere un riflesso culturale di ciรฒ che avvenne quando i Greci si sostituirono alla popolazione locale quando giunsero nel Peloponneso intorno al 1000 a.C.[PARAGRAPH:1]Oltre alle storie sugli dei, la mitologia greca รจ piena di grandi saghe che narrano le gesta di eroi, molti dei quali avevano genitori divini. I miti di Ercole, Perseo, Giasone e le storie dei protagonisti dell'Iliade furono spesso oggetto di rielaborazione da parte degli artisti greci successivi. Si trattava di uomini che interagivano spesso con gli dei, a volte superandoli. Molti di loro erano enormemente stimati dai Greci, soprattutto se "nobili" come Achille.[PARAGRAPH:1]Anche se i Greci meglio istruiti avevano i loro dubbi sul fondamento della religione ellenica, il popolo non metteva in dubbio la veridicitร delle storie. I Greci usarono la loro religione per spiegare i fenomeni naturali e le proprie origini; era fonte di orgoglio il fatto che la propria ascendenza o la fondazione della propria cittร potessero essere fatte risalire a un atto divino.[PARAGRAPH:1]Le analisi archeologiche e linguistiche suggeriscono che la religione ellenica abbia subito una pesante influenza da parte di altre religioni. Per esempio, il dio greco Zeus, il dio romano Giove, il dio germanico Tyr e il dio sanscrito Dyaus sono tutte divinitร del cielo che condividono un'origine indo-europea risalente a chissร quando nel passato. Altre divinitร furono prese in prestito o influenzate dalle religioni del vicino Oriente: Afrodite aveva una funzione simile a quella delle semitiche Ishtar e Astarte. Nel corso del periodo ellenico e anche dopo, la religione greca si diffuse nel Mediterraneo e nel Vicino Oriente, influenzando a sua volta molte religioni pre-esistenti e facendo nascere nuovi culti mistici sincretici. Anche se oggi si tratta di una religione morta, la mitologia greca ha ispirato gli artisti per migliaia di anni.
TEXT.Spanish: La religiรณn helรฉnica, tambiรฉn conocida como mitologรญa griega, comprende un cuerpo enorme de narraciones sobre los dioses y hรฉroes. Estas historias tenรญan muchos siglos de antigรผedad y, hasta la apariciรณn de la escritura en Grecia, se conservaron mediante la tradiciรณn oral. De naturaleza politeรญsta, contaba con un sinfรญn de deidades naturales locales, ademรกs de los principales dioses y diosas del Olimpo. Los dioses griegos tenรญan forma humana, pero poseรญan poderes sobrenaturales; eran engendrados por otros dioses, pero nunca envejecรญan, no podรญan morir, podรญan cruzar enormes distancias al instante, hablaban a travรฉs de los humanos con o sin su consentimiento, etc. Algunos de ellos eran los aspectos personificados de las fuerzas de la naturaleza (Helios, por ejemplo, era literalmente el dios del sol) mientras que otros poseรญan personalidades complejas y funciones mรบltiples (seguramente Apolo sea el mejor ejemplo).[PARAGRAPH:1]Todos los dioses pertenecรญan a una gran familia, pero habรญa varias generaciones diferenciadas y su interacciรณn era en muchas ocasiones violenta. Los dioses del Olimpo, por ejemplo, habรญan alcanzado su posiciรณn de predominio tras expulsar a una generaciรณn anterior de dioses y diosas (los titanes). Esto bien pudiera ser una transposiciรณn mitolรณgica de la lucha entre los griegos y la cultura anterior, a la que desplazaron cuando llegaron al Peloponeso, alrededor del 1000 a. C.[PARAGRAPH:1]Ademรกs de las historias de los dioses, la religiรณn helรฉnica estaba repleta de grandes historias sobre las hazaรฑas de los hรฉroes, muchos de los cuales estaban emparentados con los dioses. Abundan las narraciones acerca de figuras como Hรฉrcules, Perseo, Jasรณn y los personajes de la Ilรญada. Estos humanos solรญan relacionarse con los dioses y, a veces, hasta los superaban. Los griegos sentรญan gran estima por estos hรฉroes, especialmente por los personajes "nobles", como por ejemplo Aquiles.[PARAGRAPH:1]Aunque algunos griegos cultivados tenรญan sus dudas sobre la existencia de los dioses, la mayorรญa no cuestionaba la validez de las historias. Los griegos utilizaban su religiรณn para explicar el mundo natural y sus propios orรญgenes. Desde trazar los orรญgenes del propio linaje o la fundaciรณn de una ciudad hasta los actos de un dios era motivo de gran orgullo.[PARAGRAPH:1]La arqueologรญa y el anรกlisis lingรผรญstico sugieren que la religiรณn helรฉnica estuvo muy influida por otras religiones. Por poner un ejemplo, el Zeus griego, el Jรบpiter romano, el Tyr germano y el Dyaus sรกnscrito son todos dioses del cielo que comparten un origen indoeuropeo comรบn en un pasado lejano. Otros dioses procedรญan de religiones de Oriente Prรณximo (o habรญan recibido importantes influencias de ellas): Afrodita poseรญa una funciรณn muy similar a la de las diosas semรญticas Ishtar y Astartรฉ. Durante el perรญodo helenรญstico y despuรฉs de รฉl, la religiรณn griega se extendiรณ por gran parte del Mediterrรกneo y Oriente Prรณximo, lo que influyรณ en muchas de las religiones existentes y originรณ nuevos cultos mistรฉricos y sincrรฉticos. Aunque la religiรณn helรฉnica ya no cuenta con seguidores, sigue siendo una gran fuente de inspiraciรณn para artistas del mundo entero.

Dla przyk³adu bogowie tacy jak grecki Zeus, rzymski Jowisz, nordycki Tyr oraz sanskrycki Djaus, wszyscy s¹ bogami zwi¹zanymi z niebem i dziel¹ wspólne, indoeuropejskie pochodzenie w odleg³ej przesz³oœci. Inni bogowie byli czêsto zapo¿yczani z religii bliskowschodnich, b¹dŸ te¿ posiadaj¹ w nich swoich odpowiedników; Afrodyta legitymuje siê cechami bardzo przypominaj¹cymi semickie boginie Isztar oraz Asztarte. Pomimo faktu ¿e nikt ju¿ nie wierzy w prawdziwoœæ mitologii greckiej, to wci¹¿ stanowi ona inspiracjê dla artystów z ca³ego œwiata.
